description: The visible content is a digital publisher widget or interface icon rather than a scientific materials characterization figure. It shows a red bookmark symbol enclosed within concentric yellow and blue circular rings, positioned above the English text 'Check for updates.' No experimental sub-panels, micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectroscopic curves, mechanical property plots, or phase diagrams related to the Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels are present. There are no visible alloy designations, sample numbers, heat treatment labels, scale bars, or quantitative axes. Consequently, none of the microstructural constituents discussed in the paper—such as austenite, ferrite, δ-ferrite, or κ-carbide lamellae formed via discontinuous precipitation, cellular transformation, or spinodal decomposition—can be identified, and no hardness values or processing conditions can be read from the image. The figure appears to be a placeholder or webpage element unrelated to the experimental dataset, so no scientific conclusion about decomposition mechanisms can be drawn from the visual content alone.

This figure presents two optical micrographs illustrating discontinuous precipitation in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels after tempering at 600 °C for 30 min followed by water quenching. Panel (a) shows the 9Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy, where the caption states that austenite (γ) grains underwent only partial discontinuous precipitation, with γ + κ lamellar structures initiating from grain boundaries and only partially consuming the parent grains. Panel (b) depicts the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy, in which almost the entire austenite volume transformed into fine γ + κ lamellae, leaving only a few band-shaped γ regions untransformed; the caption reports that some κ-phase may also be present. The direct comparison demonstrates that increasing carbon content from 0.4 wt% to 1 wt% markedly enhances the extent and completeness of discontinuous precipitation, producing finer and more extensive lamellar colonies. This supports the central conclusion that carbon concentration provides a stronger Al–C driving force for κ-carbide precipitation and alters phase equilibria, thereby governing the kinetics of austenite decomposition during tempering, which correlates with the substantially higher reported hardness of 439 HV for the 1C grade compared to the 0.4C grade.

This figure comprises two scanning electron micrographs acquired at 20 kV and ×10,000 magnification showing discontinuous precipitation microstructures in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels at 600 °C. Subfigure (a) presents the 9Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy, where discontinuous precipitation initiates at grain boundaries and only partially transforms the austenite matrix into fine γ + κ-carbide lamellae. Subfigure (b) shows the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy, where most of the austenite has undergone discontinuous precipitation, yielding fine lamellae with short inter-lamellar spacing; caption states that the remaining non-transformed austenite may feature nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates. The side-by-side comparison reveals that raising carbon content from 0.4 wt% to 1 wt% substantially promotes the extent of discontinuous precipitation. Correspondingly, the fine lamellar structure in 9Al-10Mn-1C is associated with significant hardening (439 HV). These images support the core conclusion that carbon accelerates the formation of κ-carbide lamellae, and the resultant fine inter-lamellar spacing provides the pronounced strengthening effect.

Figure 3 comprises two secondary-electron SEM micrographs acquired at 20 kV and ×10,000 magnification (scale bar 1 μm) showing Fe–Mn–Al–C steels tempered at 600 °C. Panel (a) corresponds to 12Al-10Mn-0.4C (caption reports 2Al-10Mn-0.4C, contextually corrected to 12 % Al) and exhibits a multifaceted microstructure with irregular lamellar domains, globular precipitates, and a matrix phase. Panel (b) corresponds to 12Al-10Mn-1C and shows pronounced, uniformly oriented parallel lamellae spanning the micrograph. Citation text states that at 12 % Al the reactions involved the whole austenite volume, that 12Al-10Mn-0.4C precipitation produced a multifaceted microstructure, and that 12Al-10Mn-1C did not develop γ + κ-carbide lamellar structures but instead featured carbon-depleted austenite with globular κ-carbides, although the micrograph visually displays strong lamellar features. The figure supports the conclusion that raising Al to 12 % drives full-volume austenite transformation and yields a precipitation morphology distinct from the discontinuous γ + κ lamellae formed at 9 % Al, thereby altering the microstructural stability and hardening response.

This optical micrograph displays the precipitation transformation microstructure of a 12Al-10Mn-1C Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel after tempering at 600°C for 30 minutes. The image reveals a complex, random-like lamellar morphology that is notably less ordered than typical discontinuous precipitation, featuring two distinct annotated regions: fine γ+κ lamellae and areas containing γ+coarse κ precipitates. The microstructure consists of alternating bands of transformed austenite with fine lamellar κ-carbides and coarser κ-carbide particles dispersed in retained austenite, alongside carbon-depleted γ bands. The 100 μm scale bar indicates the mesoscale nature of these precipitate colonies. As reported in the caption and citation context, this microstructure exemplifies the 'generic precipitation transformation' observed in high-aluminum (12 wt%) alloys, where increased thermodynamic gradients between hot-rolling and tempering equilibria drive austenite instability. The figure provides direct metallographic evidence that this transformation involves austenite volume reactions producing heterogeneous κ-carbide distributions, which the authors link to slight hardening effects in the 600°C tempered condition.

Figure 5 comprises eight micrographs arranged in two rows: the upper row shows optical microscope micrographies (a–d) and the lower row shows corresponding secondary electron SEM images of four 800 °C tempered Fe–Mn–Al–C alloys: 9Al-10Mn-1C, 12Al-10Mn-1C, 12Al-20Mn-1C and 12Al-30Mn-1C. The optical images reveal the overall austenite decomposition morphologies—cellular transformation in (a)–(c) and spinodal decomposition in (d)—while the SEM micrographs provide finer detail at magnifications of ×2,500 and ×5,000 (20 kV). Caption states that (a) exhibits widespread κ + α + γ lamellar structures from cellular transformation together with thin band-shaped δ-ferrite grains and spinodally decomposed γ regions; (b) shows complete cellular transformation into coarse α + κ lamellae with wide inter-lamellar spacing; (c) features thick lath-like κ-carbide lamellae arising from cellular transformation alongside spinodal areas; and (d) displays fully spinodal decomposed austenite with nanoscale modulated structures visible in the SEM image. The figure collectively demonstrates that increasing Al and Mn content shifts the 800 °C tempering response from cellular transformation toward spinodal decomposition, governing lamellae dimensions, inter-lamellar spacing and associated hardening.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) secondary-electron images of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel alloy 9Al-10Mn-1C (wt.%) after tempering at 800 °C for 30 min, recorded at magnifications ranging from ×1,000 (10 μm scale bar) to ×10,000 (1 μm scale bar). The micrographs exhibit two distinct microstructural morphologies: fine-scale modulated or acicular precipitates within a matrix, and coarser aligned lamellar colonies consisting of lath-like carbides. The caption states that the former correspond to spinodal decomposition into austenite (γ) plus nano-sized κ-carbides, whereas the latter represent cellular transformation producing κ-carbides + ferrite (α) + residual austenite (γ) lamellar structures. The progressive zoom from low to high magnification illustrates the coexistence of nanoscale precipitated regions and micron-scale cellular lamellae, supporting the text conclusion that 9Al-10Mn-1C at 800 °C develops dual transformation microstructures responsible for outstanding hardness among the 9% Al alloys.

This scanning electron microscopy (SEM) secondary-electron image, acquired at 20 kV and ×5,000 magnification with a 5 μm scale bar, displays the microstructure of the 12Al-20Mn-1C Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steel after tempering at 800 °C for 30 min. The field of view reveals a fully decomposed austenitic structure dominated by wide, thick lath-like κ-carbide lamellae produced via cellular transformation, appearing as alternating light and dark bands. The upper-left region exhibits a finer, mesh-like contrast suggestive of spinodally decomposed austenite or a differently oriented cellular colony. Compared with the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy treated under the same conditions, the κ-carbide strips are markedly broader and thicker at 12 wt% Al, resulting in larger inter-lamellar spacing. The caption and citation text report that these coarse lamellae yield a robust hardening response (404 HV) despite the wide spacing, and that Mn-γ stabilization is balanced by the Al–C driving force for κ-carbide formation. This figure thus provides direct morphological evidence linking high-Al chemistry to thickened cellular lamellae during full austenitic decomposition at 800 °C.

This figure presents six scanning electron microscopy (SEM) secondary electron images at 20 kV, showing nano-sized κ-carbide morphologies resulting from spinodal decomposition in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels tempered at 800°C for 30 min. Subfigures (a) and (b) at ×10,000 and ×30,000 magnification depict agglomerated nano-sized κ-carbides in 9Al-10Mn-1C, exhibiting thin acicular morphologies within a medium-dense grid. Subfigures (c) and (d) at corresponding magnifications show cuboidal nano-sized κ-carbides in 12Al-20Mn-1C, displaying the densest and thickest configuration. Subfigures (e) and (f) reveal agglomerated κ-carbides in 12Al-30Mn-1C, appearing as acicular-like precipitates with the finest and least dense configuration. Scale bars indicate 1 μm and 0.5 μm respectively. The figure demonstrates that increasing Mn and Al contents significantly alters the size, morphology, and spatial density of spinodally decomposed κ-carbides, supporting the conclusion that composition strongly governs the nanoscale precipitation architecture in these alloys.

The figure displays two calculated phase volume fraction diagrams showing the molar amount of phases versus temperature (0–1300 °C) for Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels. Subplot (a) corresponds to 9Al-10Mn-0.4 and (b) to 9Al-10Mn-1C, with legends identifying BCC_A2, FCC_A1, and KAPPA_E21. In (a), BCC_A2 dominates at low temperature (~0.88–0.92 mol), remains stable until ~700 °C, then drops sharply to ~0.52 mol near 800 °C before rising again toward 1300 °C; FCC_A1 is negligible below 700 °C but increases to a peak of ~0.49 mol around 850 °C then declines; KAPPA_E21 stays near 0.05–0.10 mol before dissolving above ~800 °C. In (b), FCC_A1 starts at ~0.80 mol and decreases markedly above 700 °C; BCC_A2 rises steeply from near zero to a maximum of ~1.0 mol around 950 °C before falling; KAPPA_E21 is stable at ~0.20 mol until disappearing near 900 °C. As discussed in the caption and text, these contrasting thermodynamic gradients explain why the 0.4C alloy undergoes austenite decomposition during tempering at 600 °C but not at 800 °C, whereas the 1C alloy shows decomposition at both temperatures. The diagrams support the core conclusion that chemical composition governs phase equilibria and thus the driving force for austenite decomposition independent of kinetic considerations.

Figure 10 presents two optical micrographs comparing the microstructures of two Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels tempered at 600°C. Subfigure (a) shows the 9Al-20Mn-1C alloy, where austenite grains exhibit parallel intragranular striations with different crystallographic directions in each grain, alongside dark secondary phases lining the grain boundaries; the caption and associated text report these boundary phases are probably δ + α. Subfigure (b) displays the 9Al-30Mn-1C alloy under identical treatment, similarly revealing parallel striations within austenite grains, but with grain-boundary precipitates identified in the text as κ-carbides. Both images carry a 20 μm scale bar. The figure demonstrates that, despite the presence of these precipitates and striations, neither microstructure achieved significant hardening relative to its hot-rolled condition, in contrast to the fine lamellar morphology with short inter-lamellar spacing observed in 9Al-10Mn-1C at the same temperature, which produced pronounced strengthening. These observations support the conclusion that increasing Mn content from 20 wt% to 30 wt% changes the type of grain-boundary precipitation at 600°C while preserving the characteristic striated austenite morphology, though the nature of these striations requires further investigation.

Scanning electron micrograph (20 kV, ×5,000, scale bar 5 μm) of the 9Al-20Mn-1C Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steel after tempering at 600 °C for 30 min, revealing a thick, bright-contrast phase continuously decorating prior austenite (γ) grain boundaries. The precipitated phase appears to connect at triple junctions and shows physical continuity with the adjacent austenite grains, while some boundaries exhibit fine cracks or decohesion. The caption states these are κ-carbides along γ grain boundaries and highlights evidence of continuity with the γ grain. Contextual data indicate that this alloy develops parallel striations within austenite grains and secondary phases along boundaries without bulk austenite transformation at 600 °C. The image provides direct morphological evidence that κ-carbide precipitation preferentially nucleates and grows along grain boundaries during low-temperature tempering, supporting the observed hardness increase to 359 HV in the absence of discontinuous precipitation or cellular transformation.

Figure 12 presents equilibrium phase volume fraction diagrams as a function of temperature for Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels with compositions (a) 9Al-20Mn-1C, (b) 9Al-30Mn-1C, (c) 12Al-20Mn-1C, and (d) 12Al-30Mn-1C. Each subplot plots the mole fraction of phases—including FCC_A1 (austenite), BCC_A2 or BCC_2SL (ferrite), KAPPA_E21 (κ-carbide), and liquid—against temperature from 0 to 1300 °C. In the 9Al alloys (a, b), FCC_A1 dominates the intermediate-to-high temperature range, and caption states that the austenite fraction at 800 °C is higher than at 1150 °C, suggesting greater austenite stability at the lower tempering temperature. In contrast, the 12Al alloys (c, d) exhibit a rightward shift of the FCC_A1 field toward higher temperatures, and the text reports the reversed stability trend where the austenite fraction at 800 °C is lower than at 1150 °C. This demonstrates that increasing aluminium from 9 wt% to 12 wt% enlarges the thermodynamic driving force and explains the enhanced propensity for austenite decomposition during tempering in the higher-Al alloys.

This figure presents a comparative set of optical micrographs illustrating the austenite decomposition microstructures in three Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels after tempering at 800 °C for 30 min. Subfigure (a) for 9Al-10Mn-1C displays a predominantly lamellar structure corresponding to cellular transformation products (α + κ-carbide + residual γ lamellae), with spinodal decomposition present but subordinate due to lower Mn-stability. Subfigure (b) for 12Al-20Mn-1C also shows cellularly transformed fractions dominating over spinodally decomposed regions, featuring coarse lamellar colonies. In contrast, subfigure (c) for 12Al-30Mn-1C exhibits a fine, uniformly distributed acicular precipitation pattern throughout the entire austenitic matrix, indicating that only spinodal decomposition has involved the full austenitic volume. The progression from cellular-transformation-dominated to fully spinodal-decomposed microstructures as Mn and Al contents increase demonstrates that alloy chemistry strongly dictates the decomposition pathway at 800 °C, supporting the conclusion that Mn-stability governs the competition between these two transformation mechanisms.

Fig. 14 comprises two SEM secondary-electron micrographs of Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steel (composition 12Al-20Mn-1C) after tempering at 800 °C. The lower-magnification image (×2,500, 10 μm scale bar, 20 kV) shows δ-ferrite branches extending from cellular-transformed regions into adjacent spinodally decomposed austenite (γ) grains, illustrating a mixed microstructure. The higher-magnification image (×10,000, 1 μm scale bar, 20 kV) resolves the fine periodic modulation of the spinodal decomposition and the lamellar α + κ-carbide structure associated with the cellular transformation. The caption identifies these as δ-ferrite branches within spinodal decomposed γ grains. Together the images support the observation that, in 12Al-20Mn-1C tempered at 800 °C, cellular-transformed fractions and spinodal decomposition can coexist within the same grain, with δ-ferrite branches penetrating the spinodally decomposed austenite; the phenomenon is noted as requiring further study.
